Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty hands-on and scattered. I'd dedicate around 25 hours a week, but it wasn't like I sat at a desk reading textbooks for hours. I learned best by doing things—working through problems, building models, or practicing with actual materials. I'd often spread my studying across different locations and times, fitting it around my extracurricular activities and whatever else was going on that week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else I had going on. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, other weeks my extracurriculars would take over and I'd have to cram things in wherever I could. Deadlines were the main driver—if something big was coming up, I'd shift my whole schedule around it. I tried to be regular but life just didn't always cooperate with that plan.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check on assignments or see if there were any announcements I'd missed. Sometimes my peers would mention something in the discussion forums that made me curious, so I'd hop on to see what was happening. I also logged in to watch videos when I had downtime, though I'll admit it wasn't always the first thing on my mind. Notifications from classmates were actually a big motivator for me to check in.
Q4: Yeah, there was a stretch where logging in became pretty automatic, especially when the discussion forums got active. I had a few classmates I'd regularly interact with, and checking their posts became part of my morning routine with coffee. The peer support element really helped cement that habit. Once I started seeing familiar names and ongoing conversations, it felt more like a community than just a platform.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely useful—I watched over 230 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and really work through concepts at my own pace. The discussion forums were also great because I'm a kinesthetic learner and talking through ideas with peers helped me process things better than just reading. When someone explained something differently than the instructor, it often clicked for me in a new way.
Q6: I'll be honest, I ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. If I couldn't interact with it or apply it somehow, I'd put it off indefinitely. The more passive resources just didn't match how I learn best. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd sit in a folder untouched while I focused on things I could actually work through hands-on.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to something practical or if it involved interaction with others. Discussions where people were genuinely exchanging ideas felt valuable. Anything that felt like busywork or just checking a box—I'd mentally check out even if I technically completed it. I needed to feel like I was building something or solving something real.
Q8: Optional activities felt like opportunities rather than obligations, which actually made me more interested in some of them. Required ones sometimes felt like hoops to jump through, even if the content was good. With optional stuff, I'd pick what genuinely interested me and engage more deeply. Required activities I'd sometimes rush through just to get them done, which probably wasn't the best approach.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge chunk of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and watch them on my commute or while doing other things. I had notebooks full of diagrams and practice problems that I worked through by hand. I also used external YouTube videos and practice sites that explained things in different ways. The LMS was just one piece of a much bigger puzzle for me.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be working through problems with physical flashcards or practicing concepts with study group members in person, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd print out materials and mark them up with highlighters and sticky notes. Sometimes my best learning happened when I was explaining something to a friend over coffee—totally invisible to any tracking system.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then download it or take detailed notes and never go back to the platform version. I'd reference my notes for weeks afterward while the system thought I'd only engaged with that content once. Same with readings—I'd access them, save them locally, and work through them multiple times offline. My actual engagement was way higher than my login patterns suggested.
Q12: Around midterms there was a period where everything felt overwhelming. My extracurriculars had a big event, I was juggling five online courses, and my motivation just tanked. I'd log in and stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. The stress was medium-level but persistent, and it made it hard to focus even when I had the time. I had to lean on my resilience to push through that rough patch.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or stressed, I'd default to easier activities on the LMS—like scrolling through discussions rather than tackling actual assignments. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant I'd sometimes access materials during breaks without really engaging deeply. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while, which probably hurt my assignment completion rate.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing while I was doing something else, or I'd scroll through readings without really processing them. I was technically 'active' but mentally checked out. It usually happened when I was tired or when the material didn't grab me. Those sessions felt more like going through the motions than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was low motivation—when I wasn't excited about something, finding the energy to start was tough. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow and then tomorrow would become next week. The peer support helped because friends would check in and hold me accountable, but I still struggled with procrastination on solo work.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd have bursts of intense activity right before things were due, then go quiet for days. It wasn't ideal but it's how I operated. The pressure of a deadline actually helped me focus in ways that regular days didn't. My login frequency was pretty low overall, but when I did log in near deadlines, I was fully locked in.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback motivated me to engage more with similar content. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and move on to other things. The peer interactions in discussions provided faster feedback loops, which I found more motivating than waiting for official grades. Knowing my peers were reading my posts made me put more effort in.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which was both good and bad. Good because I could fit things around my schedule, bad because without strict structure, I'd let things slide. Having five online courses meant I had to self-manage a lot, and this course's structure didn't force me into a rhythm. I performed better when modules had clear weekly expectations rather than open-ended timelines.
Q19: The discussion forums made engagement easier because I could learn through interaction, which suits my kinesthetic style. The video content being available on-demand was also helpful. What made it harder was when content was purely text-based with no interactive elements. Also, the sheer volume of materials across five courses made it easy to deprioritize things that didn't seem immediately urgent.
Q20: I think that happens when students are genuinely trying to understand but struggling to produce output that meets the requirements. You might watch every video and read every discussion but still not feel confident enough to submit work. Or life gets in the way of the final step of actually completing assignments. High activity without submissions could also mean someone's learning in their own way but not translating it to formal assessments.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My attendance and assignment completion rates were pretty low, but I still pulled a Merit because I was learning effectively outside the system. The exam tested what I actually knew, and I'd prepared thoroughly through offline methods. LMS activity doesn't capture the quality of learning, just the quantity of clicks. I was engaged, just not always visibly.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how long you spend on things, what you click on. But they miss the depth of engagement and whether you're actually learning. They can't see me working through problems on paper or discussing concepts with friends. They show behavior, not understanding. A log might show I watched a video once, but it can't show that I thought about it for days afterward.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally invested in understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I find myself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when I'm genuinely curious about what comes next. Engagement for me is active—discussing, questioning, applying. It's not about how many times I log in; it's about whether the content is actually taking root in my brain.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned from peer conversations outside the platform, or the hours I spent with physical materials and handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture my genuine happiness when concepts clicked, or the stress I managed through while still pushing forward. My resilience, my offline study habits, my real engagement with peers—all of that is invisible to the data but central to how I actually learned.
